game_name: World Class Track Meet / Stadium Events

danger_signals.visual_form: Player lifting feet before starting signal on mat
danger_signals.meaning: Rule violation; three false starts result in disqualification from tournament

danger_signals.visual_form: White box markers appearing along screen edge during 110M hurdles event
danger_signals.meaning: Required jump zones; failure to jump at correct timing results in collision/performance loss

safety_signals.visual_form: Runners positioned at race start on stadium track
safety_signals.meaning: Ready state; awaiting 'on your mark' signal to begin competition

safety_signals.visual_form: Player successfully clearing hurdle or landing jump within marked zones
safety_signals.meaning: Correct performance; contributes to score advancement

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Computer-controlled opponent runners visually distinct on split-screen during dash and hurdle events
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Left side of mat designated for dash and hurdle events; either side for jump events

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Tournament opponent changed species between Japanese and international versions
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Six distinct computer players to defeat sequentially in tournament mode

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Regional variants show different stadium background colors (dark blue sky in Japan, light blue in US original, darkened in World Class Track Meet re-release); Japanese flags replaced with US flags in international versions; 'Mat B' graphic rebranded as 'Power Pad' in re-release
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player selects event type and registers alternating competitors (up to six per event)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Split-screen display showing player vs. computer opponent side-by-side during 100M dash and 110M hurdle events
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player must beat each of six sequential computer opponents at both dash and hurdles; three false starts trigger disqualification

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: All four events displayed consecutively; scoring references Guinness 1982 world track records as visual/textual benchmarks
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Multiple alternating players compete across 100M dash, 110M hurdles, long jump, and triple jump; cumulative scoring

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Runner animation speed correlates to which row of mat buttons player activates
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Top speed of character runner depends on button row selection; higher rows = faster acceleration

environmental_storytelling.example: Stadium background colors and sky tone changed between Japanese (dark blue), US original (light blue), and World Class Track Meet re-release (darker, less saturated). Sky color and stadium color palette communicate regional/publisher identity.

'Mat B' graphic rebranded to 'Power Pad' visual in World Class Track Meet version; all Bandai mentions scrubbed for Nintendo re-release. Publisher identity communicated through accessory branding in UI.

environmental_storytelling.visual_manifestation: Guinness 1982 world track records displayed as benchmark in Olympics mode; creates aspirational performance standard
environmental_storytelling.behavioral_manifestation: Player scores measured against real-world athletic records, establishing competitive context

environmental_storytelling.example: Stadium environment with track, hurdles, and jump zones provide authentic athletic venue context. Signage and layout communicate Olympic/championship-level competition.

entity_behavioral_tells.state: Pre-start (feet elevated on mat before signal)
entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Flagged as false start; visual/mechanical penalty applied
Speed determined by mat button row; running animation speed reflects input frequency
Must jump when white box markers appear; timing-based mechanic communicates skill requirement
Jump height and duration maintained by continued mat input; air time extends distance
Six distinct opponents appear sequentially; visual distinction indicates progression through tournament bracket

notes: World Class Track Meet is primarily a fitness/sports competition simulator with minimal narrative or faction conflict. Visual communication focuses on athletic performance metrics, event rules, and regional/corporate branding differences rather than danger/safety or political hierarchy. The game lacks traditional world-state complexity; instead, its 'states' are event-based (dash, hurdles, jumps, tournament vs. Olympics mode). Regional variants communicate publisher/market differentiation through UI and environmental color palette changes rather than gameplay-affecting world-state shifts. The mat accessory itself serves as the primary interface, with visual cues on-screen indicating required actions (hurdle markers, start signals).
sources_used: tcrf - Family Fun Fitness: Stadium Events (The Cutting Room Floor)
